resume:
Sandra Whitfield
Vice President of Operations | Supply Chain, Manufacturing & Fulfillment
Columbus, OH | sandra.whitfield@example.com | 14 years in operations leadership

EXECUTIVE PROFILE
Vice President of Operations with fourteen years of progressive leadership across manufacturing, distribution, and third-party logistics. I lead large multi-site organizations through periods of growth, cost pressure, and operational turnaround. My focus is building durable operating systems: the cadences, metrics, and leadership benches that let an organization keep improving after I move on. I have owned end-to-end operations P&L, partnered with sales and finance on capacity and margin, and represented operations in board and audit-committee settings. I am equally comfortable on the plant floor with frontline supervisors and in the boardroom defending a capital plan.

PROFESSIONAL EXPERIENCE
Vice President of Operations — Keystone Consumer Goods (2019 to present)
I own operations for a consumer-products company spanning three manufacturing plants and two distribution centers, with a combined organization of roughly 1,100 employees. I report to the COO and own the operations P&L, including labor, materials conversion cost, freight, and capital projects. During my tenure I have led a network redesign, a warehouse-management-system replacement, and an ongoing lean-transformation program across the plants. I chair the monthly operations review and sit on the company's capital committee. I have guided the organization through two demand surges and one significant downturn, including a workforce adjustment that I worked to handle with care for both the people affected and the teams that remained. I partner closely with the sales organization on demand planning and with finance on the annual operating plan. My leadership philosophy centers on clarity of ownership, simple visual metrics, and developing the next layer of leaders so that improvements stick.

Senior Director of Operations — Glenwood Distribution (2015 to 2019)
I led distribution and fulfillment for a regional 3PL serving retail and e-commerce clients, with responsibility for four facilities and approximately 600 associates. I owned service-level commitments to clients, the cost-to-serve model, and the labor-management program across the network. I led the standup of a new fulfillment center from site selection support through go-live, and I drove a multi-year safety program that I am proud of. I managed the operational side of several client onboardings and was the senior operations escalation point for our largest accounts. I worked extensively with our clients' supply-chain leaders to align on forecasts, peak planning, and continuous-improvement roadmaps.

Director of Manufacturing Operations — Birchwood Industrial (2011 to 2015)
I ran manufacturing operations for an industrial-components plant, including production planning, maintenance, quality, and continuous improvement. I led the plant's first structured lean program, established a daily management system, and built out the supervisor development pipeline. I partnered with engineering on new-product introductions and with procurement on supplier quality.

EARLY CAREER
I began in operations as a production supervisor and progressed through plant-level management roles, learning the fundamentals of frontline leadership, scheduling, and shop-floor problem solving. That grounding on the floor continues to shape how I lead: I believe operations leaders earn credibility by understanding the work, not just the spreadsheet.

SELECTED INITIATIVES
Across my roles I have sponsored a number of cross-functional initiatives that I am happy to speak to at a high level. At Keystone I served as the executive sponsor of the warehouse-management-system replacement, working with our technology and finance partners to align the program with the broader operations roadmap and to keep the organization focused through a complex transition. I have championed standardized daily management across all of our sites, with the belief that a common operating rhythm makes the whole network easier to lead and easier to improve. I have also been a consistent advocate for investing in frontline supervisor development, because in my experience the supervisor layer is where operational culture is either made or lost. On the network side, I led the executive steering of a footprint review intended to better align our manufacturing and distribution presence with where demand was growing, and I partnered with sales and finance to socialize the direction and build organizational buy-in. In each of these I see my role as the sponsor who sets direction, secures resources, and removes barriers, while empowering the leaders closest to the work to make the detailed calls.

HOW I PARTNER WITH THE EXECUTIVE TEAM
I view operations as a service to the whole enterprise, not a silo. I partner with sales on demand and capacity so that commercial commitments and operational reality stay in sync; I partner with finance on the operating plan and capital allocation so that investment follows strategy; and I partner with human resources on workforce planning, development, and the difficult moments that test an organization's values. I try to be the kind of operations leader who brings calm and clarity in a crisis and who, in steadier times, is quietly building the bench and the systems that make the next crisis less painful. I am a strong believer that the best operations leaders make themselves progressively less necessary by building organizations that improve on their own.

LEADERSHIP APPROACH
I think of my job as building an operating system rather than heroically solving problems myself. That means installing the right cadences — daily tiered huddles, weekly operating reviews, monthly business reviews — and the right metrics so that issues surface early and the organization self-corrects. It means developing leaders one and two levels down so the system outlives any individual. And it means being disciplined about ownership: every metric has a name attached, and accountability is clear. I care deeply about safety as a leading indicator of operational health, and I treat workforce decisions, including difficult ones, as a test of an organization's values.

AREAS OF EXPERTISE
Multi-site operations leadership; operations P&L ownership; lean and continuous-improvement transformation; distribution and fulfillment; warehouse-management-system implementation; network design; demand and capacity planning; safety program leadership; leadership development and succession; board and audit-committee engagement.

BOARD AND EXTERNAL
I have presented operations plans and risk reviews to boards and audit committees. I mentor early-career operations leaders through a regional professional association and have spoken at two industry operations conferences on building daily management systems.

EDUCATION
MBA, Operations and Supply Chain, The Ohio State University.
B.S. Industrial Engineering, Purdue University.

WHAT I'M LOOKING FOR
I am looking for a VP or SVP operations role where I can build a durable operating system across a growing multi-site footprint, partner deeply with the executive team, and develop the next generation of operations leaders. I do my best work in organizations that are scaling and that value both operational rigor and a humane culture.
jd: A multi-site consumer-products manufacturer is hiring a Vice President of Operations to own end-to-end operations across plants and distribution centers (roughly 1,000+ employees), reporting to the COO. You will own the operations P&L, lead a lean transformation, drive network and capacity decisions, and represent operations to the board. Requirements: 10+ years in operations leadership, multi-site P&L ownership, demonstrated turnaround or transformation results, and experience leading through both growth and contraction. This is a senior, accountability-heavy role. In interviews we go deep fast: we expect VPs to move quickly from philosophy to a specific decision they personally owned, with the metric that moved, the timeframe, and the tradeoff they made. We screen out candidates who speak only in operating-system generalities and cannot land a concrete, owned example with numbers.

history.q: You describe your job as 'building an operating system.' Let's make that concrete — give me one specific operational problem you personally owned and what you did about it.
You mentioned a network redesign at Keystone. Walk me through the actual decision — what changed, and what metric were you trying to move?
I'd like a real number and a real timeframe. Pick the network redesign or the lean program, and tell me the before, the after, and over what period.

candidate_last_answer: Right, so the way I approach something like that is always through the lens of the operating system. It's about clarity of ownership and making sure the cadences are in place so the organization can surface and solve these things itself. The network redesign was a good example of that — it was really about aligning the footprint with where the business was going and making sure we had the right metrics visibility. We engaged the right stakeholders, we built the cadence around it, and the organization responded well. I'm a big believer that if you get the system right, the numbers take care of themselves. So I'd say the outcome was a more resilient, better-aligned operation overall.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: if you get the system right, the numbers take care of themselves

expected-yield: a single concrete decision the candidate personally made in the network redesign — which sites or lanes changed, the specific metric (cost-to-serve, freight, fill rate) with a before/after number and timeframe, and the tradeoff she accepted — puncturing the operating-system abstraction she keeps retreating into
